Structured qualitative risk rating: Risk was separated into four dimensions — introduction likelihood, conditional onward-transmission likelihood after an importation, impact if detection was delayed, and confidence in the judgement — with categories treated as comparative reference classes rather than calibrated probabilities; boundary ratings such as ‘very low to low’ were retained when the evidence did not support a more precise classification. The ratings were calibrated against WHO’s 6 June assessment, which classified risk as very high in the DRC, high in Uganda and bordering countries, low in the remainder of the WHO African Region, and low globally.

Reporting-date indexing and its limitations: The Poisson counting intervals describe counting variation only and do not incorporate systematic uncertainty from backlogs, under-ascertainment, changing testing, or reporting delays. The observed increase in notification rates cannot be interpreted as a direct measure of biological acceleration, as WHO noted that expanded surveillance, testing, and diagnostic capacity contributed to rising reported counts, and newly reported cases could include backlogs.

Risk ratings as comparative reference classes rather than calibrated probabilities: Risk categories are comparative reference classes rather than calibrated probabilities. Passenger-volume and confidential readiness data were unavailable, so country ratings rest on route availability, occupational exposure patterns, and qualitative preparedness indicators rather than quantified traveller volumes.

Transmission requires direct contact: A recognised symptomatic case can usually be contained because transmission requires direct contact with blood or other body fluids, contaminated materials, or the body of a deceased case rather than ordinary casual proximity. This structural assumption underpins the low conditional sustained-transmission rating for North Africa and Europe.

Border screening ineffectiveness during incubation: An incubating traveller can cross a border while asymptomatic because people are not considered infectious before symptom onset and the incubation period can extend to 21 days; temperature screening may detect some symptomatic travellers but cannot exclude infection during incubation.

Itinerary concealment risk: A final departure airport may conceal earlier travel to eastern DRC; triage therefore requires a complete 21-day itinerary and exposure history.

Cumulative doubling time not treated as a transmission signal: Arithmetic cumulative doubling times were treated as descriptive properties of the cumulative curve and not as evidence of changes in transmission.

Dependence on public aggregate data: The main limitation is dependence on public aggregate data; reporting dates do not represent symptom onset, and the analysis could not estimate time-varying reproduction numbers, serial intervals, onset-to-isolation delays, household secondary-attack rates, health-zone-specific growth, or intervention effects.

Incomplete auxiliary inputs for importation assessment: Passenger volumes, traveller occupations, deployment rosters, medevac activity, and country-specific laboratory and referral readiness were incomplete; country ratings were therefore comparative judgements rather than calibrated probabilities.

Counting intervals understating true uncertainty: Notification rates remain composite measures of infection, care seeking, alert investigation, specimen transport, laboratory capacity, retrospective death investigation, and reporting; the exact Poisson intervals are therefore deliberately described as counting-model intervals rather than complete epidemiological uncertainty intervals.

CFR interpretation caution: The rise in confirmed-case CFR is compatible with maturation of outcomes among previously active cases, retrospective confirmation of deaths, delayed presentation, preferential detection of severe disease, delayed recovery reporting, and changes in testing or death investigation; it should not be interpreted as a time-resolved measure of clinical virulence.

Unresolved status discrepancy in DRC counts: The published status totals did not fully reconcile — 828 deaths, 390 recoveries, and 725 hospitalised patients summed to 1,943, leaving 181 of 2,124 confirmed cases outside those categories — and public reports did not define the status of these cases; potential explanations include pending admission or outcome reconciliation, transfers, and reporting lags, but no category could be assigned without line-list data.

Time-stamped analysis: The analysis was time-stamped and may be superseded by subsequent official reports.

Primary outbreak data: Public surveillance reports from WHO, ECDC, Africa CDC, national ministries of health, and peer-reviewed sources were synthesised through 15–17 July 2026. The main DRC trajectory used laboratory-confirmed cases and deaths among confirmed cases; suspected, probable, and confirmed categories were not summed across reports because individuals and deaths can be reclassified as investigations progress. Uganda’s official dashboard as of 17 July reported 20 confirmed cases, two confirmed deaths, 18 recoveries, and no current admissions. WHO’s jurisdictional accounting on 17 July comprised 2,145 confirmed cases globally: 2,124 in the DRC, 20 in Uganda, and one in France, with 830 confirmed-case deaths.

Auxiliary data: Peer-reviewed primary studies were used for historical BDBV outbreaks, viral persistence, and candidate countermeasures. Airline sources were used only to establish the existence of plausible routes and not to estimate passenger volumes. ECDC Monte Carlo importation summaries (approximately one importation per 23,000–24,000 travellers, with 90% uncertainty interval one per 13,000–54,000) were referenced as separately generated estimates and were not treated as exact reciprocals.

hierarchy.lvl3: Gashema & Muvunyi (2026) Can stochastic modelling predict cross-border spread of the Bundibugyo Ebola virus outbreak in Africa?
Gashema & Muvunyi (2026) Can stochastic modelling predict cross-border spread of the Bundibugyo Ebola virus outbreak in Africa?

records.content: Read the report

A modelling study by Dick Chamla and colleagues uses fitted stochastic SEIRD (susceptible, exposed, infectious, recovered, deceased) ensemble modelling to leverage limited data to predict cross-border outbreak spread and inform regional preparedness. The analysis estimates the probability of regional spillover to neighbouring countries, finding a high probability of spread to Uganda (94.2%) and South Sudan (69.3%), while projected risks for Rwanda (8.6%) and Burundi (2.0%) remain comparatively low. By linking outbreak dynamics with spillover risk, the analysis moves beyond forecasting disease burden toward informing operational preparedness.

The central estimates of regional spillover probability are: Uganda 94.2%, South Sudan 69.3%, Rwanda 8.6%, and Burundi 2.0%. These are presented as point estimates of spillover probability rather than case counts or incidence figures. No uncertainty bounds, confidence intervals, or sensitivity ranges around these probabilities are reported in this document. No outbreak size estimate (case count or incidence) is provided.

Fitted stochastic SEIRD ensemble modelling: The analysis describes a stochastic susceptible, exposed, infectious, recovered, and deceased (SEIRD) ensemble modelling approach developed by Chamla and colleagues, fitted to limited outbreak data to predict cross-border outbreak spread. The method produces country-level spillover probability estimates for Uganda, South Sudan, Rwanda, and Burundi. The method is described only at a high level in this commentary; no calibration procedure, specific parameter values, or detailed description of how mobility or epidemiological inputs are incorporated are provided in the document reviewed here.

Homogeneous mixing and uniform intervention effectiveness: The stochastic models assume homogeneous population mixing and uniform intervention effectiveness, potentially overlooking important spatial, health-care, and socioeconomic heterogeneities that shape transmission dynamics.

Data quality and mobility approximations: Projections are constrained by the quality of early surveillance data and approximated mobility patterns, particularly in low- or limited-resource settings where data gaps are common.

Static behavioural assumptions: The models are limited in their ability to capture behavioural adaptation and community engagement, including changes in risk perception, health-care-seeking behaviour, intervention uptake, and public trust, all of which can substantially influence outbreak trajectories.

Detection delay: An estimated 6-week interval between the probable index case and formal outbreak declaration is noted, representing an undetected transmission window that shapes the model’s starting conditions.

Incomplete information during evolving outbreaks: Models developed during unfolding outbreaks inevitably operate under conditions of incomplete information, evolving surveillance systems, and changing response capacities, which can affect their ability to accurately predict transmission dynamics. Accordingly, these models are not intended to provide a precise forecast, but rather to identify reasonable transmission trajectories and inform preparedness decisions.

Homogeneous mixing assumption: Stochastic models assume homogeneous population mixing and uniform intervention effectiveness, potentially overlooking important spatial, health-care, and socioeconomic heterogeneities that shape transmission dynamics.

Data quality and mobility data gaps: Projections are further constrained by the quality of early surveillance data and approximated mobility patterns, particularly in low- or limited-resource settings where data gaps are common.

Behavioural dynamics not captured: The models remain restricted in their ability to capture behavioural adaptation and community engagement, including changes in risk perception, health-care-seeking behaviour, intervention uptake, and public trust, all of which can substantially influence outbreak trajectories.

Scenario-based estimates requiring continuous refinement: These limitations underscore the importance of interpreting model outputs as scenario-based estimates that require continuous refinement as more comprehensive epidemiological, mobility, and behavioural data become available.

The document does not specify the primary outbreak data sources or auxiliary data inputs used in the Chamla and colleagues modelling study in sufficient detail to enumerate them. The study is described as integrating epidemiological, laboratory, and mobility data into real-time risk assessments. Projections are noted to be constrained by early surveillance data and approximated mobility patterns, particularly in low- or limited-resource settings where data gaps are common, but specific dataset names, time periods covered, or sample sizes are not provided in this commentary.

hierarchy.lvl3: Chamla et al. (2026) Size of the 2026 Ebola outbreak and risk of cross-border spillover from Bundibugyo virus in Ituri Province, DR Congo, and its implications for preparedness: a recalibrated stochastic modelling study

Chamla et al. (2026) Size of the 2026 Ebola outbreak and risk of cross-border spillover from Bundibugyo virus in Ituri Province, DR Congo, and its implications for preparedness: a recalibrated stochastic modelling study

records.content: Read the report

This analysis aims to establish a short-term epidemic trajectory of the 2026 BDBV outbreak and quantify the probability of cross-border spillover into neighbouring countries at elevated importation risk, to guide preparedness priorities. It uses a stochastic SEIRD ensemble model calibrated to laboratory-confirmed DRC cases, anchored at 598 cumulative confirmed cases on 8 June 2026, linked to a daily-hazard spillover model estimating importation probability for Uganda, South Sudan, Rwanda, and Burundi over a 12-week horizon. Under the central scenario (R0 = 1.71), the model projects a median of 990 cumulative confirmed cases by week 12 (24 June 2026; 90% PI 709 to 1,293) and 174 deaths, with cross-border importation probabilities of 94.2% for Uganda, 69.3% for South Sudan, 8.6% for Rwanda, and 2.0% for Burundi.

Central scenario (R0 = 1.71): The ensemble projects a median of 648 cumulative confirmed cases by week 10 (8 June 2026; 50% PI 560 to 732), rising to 990 by week 12 (24 June 2026; 50% PI 858 to 1,141 and 90% PI 709 to 1,293), with a projected median of 174 deaths at week 12. By week 24, the central scenario projects a median of 8,210 cumulative confirmed cases (50% PI 6,636 to 10,287 and 90% PI 5,063 to 13,498) and approximately 1,440 deaths if transmission is merely sustained.

Low-transmissibility scenario (R0 = 1.42): Cumulative confirmed cases reach a median of 870 by week 12 (50% PI 752 to 1,004 and 90% PI 641 to 1,133; 160 deaths) and 3,662 by week 24 (50% PI 2,884 to 4,484 and 90% PI 2,094 to 6,037; 673 deaths).

High-transmissibility scenario (R0 = 2.08): Confirmed cases reach a median of 1,364 (50% PI 1,154 to 1,579 and 90% PI 975 to 1,807; 202 deaths) by week 12, rising to 66,420 (50% PI 56,560 to 78,937 and 90% PI 41,718 to 95,881; 10,644 deaths) by week 24.

Because the observed confirmed-case trajectory is consistent with the central scenario, the high scenario is not the operative trajectory; it is retained as an upper-bound contingency should control measures lapse.

Spillover estimates: Cross-border importation probabilities over the 12-week horizon are: Uganda 94.2%; South Sudan 69.3%; Rwanda 8.6%; and Burundi 2.0%.

Stochastic SEIRD ensemble model: A discrete-time stochastic SEIRD (susceptible, exposed, infectious, recovered, dead) framework using binomial draws under standard mass-action incidence, applied to the affected health zones in Ituri and North Kivu provinces. No vaccinated compartment is included, as no licensed BDBV-specific vaccine exists. Three transmissibility scenarios are modelled (low R0 = 1.42; central R0 = 1.71; high R0 = 2.08), with response-intensity reductions applied from 15 May 2026 of 20% (low), 25% (central), and 15% (high). Calibration was performed by simulation filtering (approximate rejection sampling, not formal Bayesian inference), accepting replicates if cumulative laboratory-confirmed cases at day 68 fell within ±30% of 598 (range 419 to 777); 400 calibration-passing replicates per scenario were retained.

Daily-hazard spillover model: Importation risk to Uganda, South Sudan, Rwanda, and Burundi was modelled as a daily Poisson hazard driven by calibrated infectious prevalence, country-specific cross-border mobility, point-of-entry detection sensitivity from WHO Joint External Evaluation assessments, and travel-while-infectious parameters (Uganda 12%; South Sudan 15%; Rwanda and Burundi 8%), with full equations and parameter values given in appendix 3. The importation probability for each country is computed as 1 − exp(cumulative 12-week hazard), yielding the probability of at least one importation over the horizon. Sensitivity analyses examined travel-while-infectious (5% to 25%), reporting completeness (0.60 to 0.90), and catchment size (250,000 to 500,000).

Transmissibility (R0): Three scenarios span published BDBV estimates from the 2007 Uganda and 2012 DR Congo outbreaks: low R0 = 1.42, central R0 = 1.71, and high R0 = 2.08. The confirmed-case trajectory is described as most consistent with the central scenario.

Delay distributions: The latent period is fixed at 9.7 days and the infectious period at 5.0 days, in line with the broader filovirus literature.

Case-fatality ratio: Drawn from a uniform distribution (0.15 to 0.25), centred on the observed confirmed-case ratio of approximately 19% (115 of 598 as of 8 June 2026) and consistent with the historical BDBV range.

Reporting fraction: Set at 1.0 for laboratory-confirmed cases, on the basis that laboratory-confirmed cases are by definition ascertained; no separate suspected-case reporting fraction is assumed.

Response effectiveness: The model incorporates a transmission reduction factor representing the combined effects of IPC measures, community engagement, safe and dignified burial, and contact tracing, with reduction factors varying by scenario to reflect differing operational effectiveness from the date of outbreak declaration onward. The response-intensity factor was applied uniformly from 15 May, though real-world response effectiveness varies by place, time, access, and community acceptance.

Spatial structure: The model assumes a homogeneously mixing catchment of 350,000 across seven affected health zones; spatial heterogeneity is not captured, which is stated as a limitation.

Cross-border spillover parameters: Importation hazard is driven by calibrated infectious prevalence, country-specific cross-border mobility, WHO Joint External Evaluation–derived point-of-entry detection sensitivity, and travel-while-infectious parameters fixed at 12% for Uganda, 15% for South Sudan, and 8% for Rwanda and Burundi. These parameters were not empirically derived for this outbreak and represent the principal sources of uncertainty in spillover projections. Varying travel-while-infectious from 5% to 25% changes Uganda’s importation probability by less than 5 percentage points (saturating at the upper end) but alters South Sudan’s projected importation probability by approximately 20 percentage points.

Calibration procedure: Simulation filtering (approximate rejection sampling) is used rather than formal Bayesian inference; the ±30% tolerance window was chosen pragmatically given residual data uncertainty.

Spatial homogeneity and model structure: Homogeneous mixing oversimplifies spatial heterogeneity across health zones; healthcare-worker transmission was not separately modelled; and response intensity was applied uniformly across the catchment rather than varying by subgeography or population group.

Single-point calibration anchor: The model is anchored on a single recent confirmed-case datum (598 on 8 June 2026), making the fit sensitive to that anchor value.

Informal inference procedure: Simulation filtering is not formal inference, and informal cross-border mobility estimates are imprecise.

Response uniformity: The response-intensity factor was applied uniformly from 15 May, but real-world response effectiveness varies by place, time, access, and community acceptance. Live mobility data and behavioural adaptation were not incorporated.

Surveillance and reporting dynamics: Post-declaration case-finding surges probably contributed to rapid apparent growth between 15 and 19 May; the authors addressed this by lowering the reporting fraction and widening the calibration window, but residual confounding between transmission intensity and surveillance intensity remains.

Early data quality: Calibration relied on early-phase surveillance counts from the first weekly WHO External Situation Report (data as of 18 May), where the cumulative suspected-case figure of 528 was only partially resolved into 35 laboratory-confirmed cases, and the death count of 132 reflected surveillance signals rather than laboratory-confirmed mortality alone. Misclassification, delayed laboratory throughput, and ongoing active case finding could each shift the calibration anchor in either direction.

Exploratory rather than definitive projections: The projections require iterative recalibration as new data emerge, and should be interpreted as an early situational assessment rather than a definitive forecast.

Absence of sex and gender disaggregation: Individual-level sex and gender data were not available in the aggregate surveillance reports, making disaggregated analysis by sex or gender impossible.

Primary outbreak data: Case data were taken from WHO AFRO External Situation Reports and the WHO AFRO internal tracker through to 8 June 2026. The laboratory-confirmed case series used as the primary calibration metric rose from 33 cases on 18 May 2026 to 105 on 24 May 2026, and 598 on 8 June 2026. Uganda reported seven confirmed cases (three imported; four locally acquired, including three healthcare workers) as of 24 May 2026. As of 4 June 2026, Uganda reported 19 confirmed cases, including five healthcare-worker infections and two deaths.

Auxiliary data: Country-specific point-of-entry detection sensitivity was drawn from WHO Joint External Evaluation assessments. The three R0 values (1.42, 1.71, 2.08) were chosen to span the range of published BDBV estimates from the 2007 Uganda and 2012 DR Congo outbreaks. The latent period (9.7 days) and infectious period (5.0 days) were taken from the broader filovirus literature. The case-fatality ratio was drawn from a uniform distribution (0.15 to 0.25), centred on the confirmed-case ratio observed in the current outbreak (approximately 19%; 115 of 598 as of 8 June 2026) and consistent with the historical BDBV range. Travel-while-infectious and cross-border mobility parameters were not empirically derived for this outbreak and represent the principal sources of uncertainty in spillover projections.

hierarchy.lvl3: Walekhwa et al. (2026) Network-based modelling of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease importation and spread in Uganda using Displacement Tracking Matrix flow data
Walekhwa et al. (2026) Network-based modelling of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease importation and spread in Uganda using Displacement Tracking Matrix flow data

records.content: Read the report

This analysis aims to quantify district-level importation and spread risk of the 2026 Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak in Uganda, originating from the ongoing DRC epidemic, in order to inform where surveillance and response resources should be concentrated. It constructs a data-driven directed weighted mobility network from IOM Displacement Tracking Matrix flows (11,245 observed movements, 15–24 May 2026) and the 2024 Uganda census, then runs a stochastic metapopulation SEIR model over 90 days across 135 Ugandan districts and two DRC provinces. Under the containment scenario, the model projects a median of 22 cumulative cases (95% CrI: 22–24) and 2 deaths concentrated entirely in Kampala over 90 days, with all other 134 districts projecting a median of 0 cases, and no statistically significant reduction from adding non-pharmaceutical interventions at any compliance level.

Under the containment scenario (effective R = 0.35), the model projected a median of 22 cumulative cases (95% CrI: 22–24) and 2 deaths (95% CrI: 2–4) over 90 days, all concentrated in Kampala, with 3 HCW infections and 0 HCW deaths; 95% credible intervals overlapped substantially across compliance scenarios, indicating no statistically significant difference between them.

Kampala was the only district with a non-zero median projection, bearing a predicted burden of 22 cases (95% CrI: 22–24) and a 100% probability of a local outbreak (>10 cases); all other 134 districts had a median of 0 predicted cases, with upper credible intervals extending to 1 in only a small fraction of simulations.

The three compliance scenarios corresponded to the following parameterisations: containment (effective R₀ = 0.35, mobility scale 0.05), moderate compliance (R₀ = 0.40, mobility scale 0.08), and low compliance (R₀ = 0.45, mobility scale 0.12). Across all three scenarios, the median total case count remained 22, with no scenario producing a statistically distinct outcome.

Directed weighted mobility network: Both undirected and directed weighted networks were built from DTM flow data using the igraph package in R; for the directed network, original flow directions were preserved to allow separate computation of in-strength (import risk) and out-strength (export risk) for each node, covering all 135 Ugandan districts and two DRC provinces. To impute 12-month movement estimates from the 10-day observed flows, observed weights were multiplied by 36.5, and uncertainty from the limited sampling window was assessed via bootstrap resampling with 1,000 replicates.

Stochastic metapopulation SEIR model: A discrete-time stochastic SEIR model is maintained at the node (district) level, with five compartments — Susceptible, Exposed, Infectious, Recovered, Dead — a latent period of 7.5 days, and an infectious period of 8.5 days. Pre-symptomatic transmission is modelled by setting the relative infectiousness of exposed individuals to 0.5 compared to infectious individuals, based on estimates from early EVD outbreaks and the empirical observation that six secondary cases in Kampala tested positive while under quarantine. The baseline transmission rate was derived from R₀ = 2.76, calibrated to the early growth of the outbreak (16 cases in 21 days). DRC provinces were modelled as explicit nodes with their own SEIR dynamics, recalibrated to actual reported data as of May 25, 2026 (381 confirmed cases, 2,987 exposed contacts, and 64 deaths). The model was run for three NPI compliance scenarios (20%, 40%, 60%) with 1,000 stochastic simulations each. The 7-1-7 agile response was explicitly modelled with dynamic contact tracing coverage (40% → 70% → 85%).

Sobol global sensitivity analysis: To quantify the influence of parameter uncertainty on total cumulative case count, a global sensitivity analysis using the Sobol method was performed, with first-order indices measuring variance explained by each parameter alone and total-order indices measuring variance including all interactions. The design used quasi-random Sobol sequences with N = 500 samples (6,500 model evaluations total), with each parameter set averaged over 3 stochastic replicates to reduce noise, and Sobol indices estimated with 200 bootstrap replicates for 95% confidence intervals.

Mobility data representativeness and scaling: The DTM flow data covering a 10-day period are assumed to be representative of average daily movement patterns after scaling by 36.5 to impute annual estimates. Movement is assumed independent each day (no memory), with only exposed and infectious individuals moving, and the probability of moving identical for both compartments. The analysis acknowledges that movements through unofficial crossings or after the outbreak escalated may not be captured, a limitation common to mobility-based epidemiological studies.

Transmission parameters: The latent period is fixed at 7.5 days and the infectious period at 8.5 days. The baseline transmission rate is derived from R₀ = 2.76, calibrated to the early growth of the outbreak (16 cases in 21 days). Pre-symptomatic infectiousness is set at 0.5 relative to fully infectious individuals, based on estimates from early EVD outbreaks and the observation that six secondary cases tested positive while under quarantine.

Case fatality and healthcare worker attack rates: The CFR (2/20 = 0.10) and HCW attack rate (3/20 = 0.15) are assumed constant throughout the simulation. The DRC CFR is fixed at 64/381 = 0.168, based on reported outbreak data.

Detection and isolation: A detection delay of 3 days is assumed to apply uniformly to all infected individuals, and quarantine after detection is assumed perfect — isolated individuals no longer transmit. Reported incidence uses a detection probability of p_detect = 1.0, assuming all symptomatic cases are detected.

DRC epidemic independence: The DRC epidemic curve is assumed to be independent of Uganda’s interventions and to drive importations solely through the movement matrix. DRC response parameters reflect a moderately delayed response: detection delay 7 days (based on the 2018–2020 DRC outbreak), notification delay 3 days, response delay 5 days, contact tracing coverage 30%, detection probability 0.7, and no behavioural response.

NPI compliance and homogeneity: Compliance with NPIs is assumed constant over the 90 days and homogeneous across districts.

Geographic scope of importations: No importations from countries other than DRC are considered.

Superspreading: New infections are drawn from a negative binomial distribution to capture superspreading, with dispersion parameter k_disp = 0.3.

Homogeneous mixing within districts: The homogeneous mixing assumption within districts likely overestimates transmission in low-density areas and underestimates it in high-density slums.

Mobility data coverage and temporal representativeness: The DTM data cover only a 10-day window and eight border crossing points; movements through unofficial crossings or after the outbreak escalated may not be captured. While annual estimates are imputed using time-dependent decay, real mobility patterns can change unpredictably.

Homogeneous mixing within districts: The homogeneous mixing assumption within districts likely overestimates transmission in low-density areas and underestimates it in high-density slums.

Unstable Sobol confidence intervals from small outbreak size: The Sobol confidence intervals for some parameters remained wide because the small outbreak size (median 22 cases) produces low output variance, making precise quantitative ranking unstable; the authors therefore interpret the Sobol results qualitatively as ranking parameter importance rather than providing precise quantitative estimates.

Hospital and funeral transmission not explicitly modelled: Hospital and funeral transmission are not explicitly modelled, a limitation given that Legrand et al. (2007) identified nosocomial transmission and unsafe burials as major amplifiers in Ebola outbreaks.

No importations from countries other than DRC: Importations from neighbouring countries other than DRC were not considered, though the 79.9% importation risk estimated by Chalma et al. (2026) focused specifically on DRC-origin introductions.

Primary outbreak data: The Uganda Ministry of Health EVD situation report as of June 5, 2026 provided initial outbreak conditions: 15 cumulative cases (plus 1 probable), 2 deaths, 5 recoveries, 720 cumulative contacts listed (455 active under follow-up), with all initial cases located in Kampala under institutional quarantine. DRC model initialisation used actual reported data as of May 25, 2026 — 381 confirmed cases, 2,987 exposed contacts, and 64 deaths — with Nord-Kivu initialised at 4,000 exposed and 400 infectious individuals, and Ituri at 500 exposed and 50 infectious.

Auxiliary data — mobility: The IOM DTM Flow Monitoring report covering 15–24 May 2026 summarised cross-border movements from DRC into Uganda and internal movements among Ugandan districts; 11,245 total observed individuals were distributed across eight Flow Monitoring Points: Ntoroko Main (28%), Mpondwe (18%), Busunga (18%), Bunagana (12%), Cyanika (8%), Goli (8%), Vurra (3%), and Busanza (3%).

Auxiliary data — demographic: The official 2024 Uganda Population and Housing Census final report (UBOS, December 2024) provided the de facto population for each of the 135 districts, totalling 45,905,417.

Auxiliary data — parameters from prior outbreaks and literature: Previous BVD outbreaks in Uganda (2007–08; 131 confirmed cases, 42 deaths) and DRC (2012; 38 confirmed cases, 13 deaths) informed background on transmissibility and CFR, with R₀ estimates ranging from 1.2 to 2.6. Bangelesa et al. contributed Flowminder-based mobility intensity index data for Ituri Province (March 2025–March 2026), covering all 340 DRC health zones; Ituri had the second highest average MII nationally (25.3), with epicentre zones Rwampara (68.6), Bunia (62.6), and Mongbwalu (49.9) all exceeding the 95th national percentile.

hierarchy.lvl3: Luvande, Iroungou, & Valdano (2026) Introduction and sustained-transmission risk across DRC health zones during the Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak
Luvande, Iroungou, & Valdano (2026) Introduction and sustained-transmission risk across DRC health zones during the Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak

records.content: Read the report

This analysis estimates introduction risk and sustained-transmission potential for each health zone (HZ) across the DRC during the ongoing Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak, with the goal of identifying priority zones — including those far from currently affected areas — where rapid amplification could follow an introduction and where response efforts should be focused. The approach applies a published method to rank all DRC health zones by their sustained-transmission potential, using population and mobility data to parameterise spatial transmission across a broad range of assumptions on disease transmissibility and contact patterns, then compares these ranks with introduction-risk ranks estimated from mobility data. The main finding is that eight health zones combine both high introduction risk and high sustained-transmission potential: Binza Ozone and Binza Meteo in Kinshasa; Karisimbi in Nord-Kivu; Kadutu and Ibanda in Sud-Kivu; and Makiso-Kisangani, Kabondo, and Mangobo in Tshopo.

This analysis does not report outbreak size estimates (e.g., total case counts or incidence figures). Introduction risk was highest in health zones close to the reported foci — specifically in Ituri, Nord-Kivu, and Haut-Uele — consistent with expected mobility patterns, with non-negligible introduction risk also appearing in distant urban centres including the Kinshasa area. High sustained-transmission potential was distributed broadly across DRC, including Kinshasa, Kasaï-Oriental, Kongo-Central, Tshopo, Nord-Kivu, Haut-Katanga, Sud-Kivu, Kasaï, and Kasaï-Central — several of which are far from the current outbreak. Crossing the two rankings identified eight health zones combining high introduction risk and high sustained-transmission potential: Binza Ozone and Binza Meteo in Kinshasa; Karisimbi in Nord-Kivu; Kadutu and Ibanda in Sud-Kivu; and Makiso-Kisangani, Kabondo, and Mangobo in Tshopo. No point probabilities, quantitative thresholds, or numerical uncertainty intervals are reported in the main text.

Sustained-transmission potential ranking: This method applies the approach developed in a previously published reference (Ref. 2) to estimate, for each DRC health zone, the potential for an introduced case to generate sustained transmission. Population and mobility data are used to parameterise spatial transmission, and a broad range of assumptions on disease transmissibility and on how local and between-community contact patterns can be inferred from these data are tested. Population data come from WorldPop and mobility data from FlowMinder.

Introduction-risk ranking: Introduction risk is estimated separately for each DRC health zone using mobility data, and health zones are ranked by this measure. Risk rankings — rather than raw risk estimates — are emphasised because they are robust across scenarios and assumptions, allowing them to inform action despite limited knowledge of transmissibility and contact patterns.

The two rankings (sustained-transmission potential and introduction risk) are then crossed to identify health zones that score highly on both dimensions. The method is described only briefly in the main text; full methodology, additional data sources, and additional results are stated to be in an Appendix.

Transmissibility and contact patterns: The analysis tests a broad range of assumptions on disease transmissibility and on how local and between-community contact patterns can be inferred from population and mobility data. Specific parameter values are not reported in the main text and are described as available in the Appendix.

Ranking robustness: A structural assumption central to the analysis is that risk rankings — as opposed to raw risk estimates — are robust across scenarios and assumptions, and that this robustness justifies using rankings to inform action despite limited knowledge of transmissibility and contact patterns. This is stated as a key design choice rather than a tested sensitivity.

Data availability: The analysis is explicit that it operates under limited data on human mobility, contact patterns, disease transmissibility, and tracing and isolation capacity; these constraints shape what can be estimated and drive the reliance on ranking rather than point estimates.

Mobility and population data: Spatial transmission is parameterised using WorldPop population data and FlowMinder mobility data. No statement is made about the vintage, resolution, or representativeness of these datasets for the current outbreak context.

Limited input data: The analysis is explicit that it operates under limited data on human mobility, contact patterns, disease transmissibility, and tracing and isolation capacity, all of which are required to fully characterise introduction and sustained-transmission risk.

Structural outbreak challenges not modelled: Insecurity, high population mobility, limited healthcare capacity, and the absence of a vaccine are cited as challenges to response. The analysis does not indicate whether these factors are formally incorporated into the model or treated as qualitative context.

Heterogeneous preparedness: The analysis acknowledges heterogeneous healthcare access, accessibility constraints, and low-to-moderate insecurity outside Kinshasa as factors affecting response feasibility in identified high-risk zones, but these are discussed qualitatively rather than incorporated into the quantitative framework.

Appendix-dependent detail: Methodology, additional data sources, and additional results are available only in the Appendix, which is not reproduced in the main document. The main text provides insufficient detail to assess specific parameter choices, calibration procedures, or sensitivity analyses.

Primary outbreak data: The analysis references the ongoing BVD outbreak in Ituri province, DRC, with confirmed cases in Nord-Kivu and Sud-Kivu noted as of June 4 as evidence of geographic spread beyond the initial focus. No surveillance line list, case count, or situation report data are described as direct model inputs in the main text.

Auxiliary data: Population data are from WorldPop (

worldpop.org) and mobility data are from FlowMinder (

flowminder.org). The sustained-transmission potential method is drawn from a previously published reference (Ref. 2), used as the methodological foundation. Additional data sources are stated to be available in the Appendix, which is not reproduced in the main document. No travel volume sample sizes, demographic inputs, or epidemiological parameters (e.g., CFR, serial interval) are reported in the main text.

hierarchy.lvl3: Kinoshita, Suzuki, & Yoneoka (2026) Early assessment of potential airline-mediated importation risk during the 2026 DRC-Uganda Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak
Kinoshita, Suzuki, & Yoneoka (2026) Early assessment of potential airline-mediated importation risk during the 2026 DRC-Uganda Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak

records.content: Read the report

This analysis projects potential airline-mediated importation risk during the 2026 BVD outbreak in DRC and Uganda, using a contemporary airline network dataset and an importation hazard parameter calibrated from historical Ebola outbreak data. The approach converts airline passenger-flow connectivity into country-level arrival probabilities over a defined time horizon. The main finding identifies major international hub countries — Belgium, France, South Africa, Kenya, and the United Arab Emirates — as the highest-probability destinations for potential importation until the end of June 2026.

This analysis produces importation probability estimates rather than outbreak size estimates. Airport-level arrival probabilities, interpreted as the probability of at least one airline-mediated importation until the end of June 2026 (t = 44), ranged from 0.247 to 0.937, with a mean of 0.431. Higher arrival probabilities were concentrated among airports with shorter effective distances from FIH and stronger connectivity through major transit hubs: for example, 0.889 at Brussels Airport, 0.888 at Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport, and 0.871 at Johannesburg O.R. Tambo International Airport. The highest country-level arrival probabilities were observed for Belgium, France, South Africa, Kenya, and the United Arab Emirates, with the top-ranked countries all exceeding 0.77; in sensitivity analyses the probabilities for these countries ranged from 0.38 to 0.98. When the starting airport is changed to EBB (Uganda) in sensitivity analysis, the mean (standard deviation) of arrival probability ranged from 0.170 (0.049) to 0.616 (0.099).

Effective-distance airline importation model: Potential airline-mediated importation risk was projected using a hazard-based model informed by contemporary airline transportation networks and a hazard parameter calibrated from the 2013–2016 West African Ebola virus disease epidemic, one of the few EBOD outbreaks with documented long-distance airline-mediated international exportations. A directed, weighted air-traffic network was constructed using airport-level origin-destination passenger-flow data from OAG for May 2024, comprising 11,264 airports, 569,741 routes, and 329,326,078 passengers. The shortest-path effective distance from FIH to each destination airport was computed using Dijkstra’s algorithm on the directed network. The arrival hazard at each destination airport was modelled as λⱼ = θ/Dⱼ (where θ = 0.19, the primary hazard parameter), and the arrival probability within t days was computed as pⱼ(t) = 1 − exp(−tλⱼ). Country-level arrival risk was summarized as the maximum airport-level probability within each country. Sensitivity analyses tested θ = {0.06, 0.32} based on the 95% confidence interval of the historical parameter estimate. An additional sensitivity analysis varied the effective-distance scaling parameter δ using epidemic growth rates r = {0.034, 0.05, 0.1} estimated as of 17 May 2026.

Transferability of historical hazard parameter: The model assumes that the relationship between effective distance and EVD importation observed during the 2013–2016 West African epidemic is transferable to the current BVD outbreak. The estimated effective distance distribution was reported as very similar to that of the previous study, which the authors cite as supporting use of the same θ value on a comparable hazard scale.

Effective-distance scaling: In the main analysis, the distance-scaling parameter δ is set to 1, assuming each additional travel step contributes one unit of distance (the Brockmann-Helbing scale), which has been empirically shown to predict global spread patterns of infectious diseases. An alternative δ formulation incorporating the epidemic growth rate and outbound passenger flow from FIH is tested in sensitivity analysis, but the authors note this increases uncertainty.

Hazard parameter sensitivity: The primary hazard value θ = 0.19 is varied to {0.06, 0.32} (the 95% CI bounds of the historical estimate) in sensitivity analyses. These sensitivity runs change absolute arrival probabilities but broadly preserve the overall geographic pattern, because effective distance determines the spatial pattern while the hazard parameter mainly scales the probabilities.

Static airline network: The estimates rely on 2024 airline network data and therefore may not capture subsequent changes in routes, passenger volumes, airspace restrictions, or travel advisories, including those affecting routes through Russia and the Middle East.

No post-PHEIC interventions: The projections do not explicitly account for post-PHEIC interventions, enhanced screening, or behavioural changes.

Source airport scope: The analysis models global air travel originating from FIH as the primary source, treating EBB only in sensitivity analysis, and does not explicitly account for land-based spread within DRC or cross-border movements to neighbouring countries.

Importation versus transmission: Estimated arrival probability is interpreted as importation risk rather than the risk of local transmission after arrival, on the basis that EBOD requires direct contact with infectious body fluids or contaminated materials, and that timely detection, isolation, contact tracing, and infection prevention and control can reduce onward transmission.

Reliance on a borrowed, cross-pathogen hazard parameter: Because no airline-mediated importation had been reported as of 28 May 2026, the hazard parameter was estimated from the 2013–2016 West African EVD epidemic rather than from current BVD data, and the model assumes this historical relationship is transferable to the present outbreak.

Static airline data and absence of intervention effects: The projections rely on May 2024 airline network data and therefore may not capture subsequent route changes, passenger volume shifts, airspace restrictions, travel advisories, or the effect of post-PHEIC interventions, enhanced screening, and behavioural changes.

Geographic scope limited to air travel from two airports: The analysis focuses on global air travel originating from FIH, with EBB considered only in sensitivity analysis, and does not explicitly account for land-based spread within DRC or cross-border movements to neighbouring countries.

Importation risk only, not local transmission risk: Estimated arrival probability should be interpreted as importation risk rather than the risk of local transmission after arrival. Countries with high-probability airport gateways should therefore be interpreted as priorities for awareness and preparedness, rather than as locations where local outbreaks are expected.

Increased uncertainty in alternative δ formulation: The sensitivity analysis δ formulation incorporates the epidemic growth rate and outbound passenger flow, but the authors note that while this adds information about the current epidemic situation, it also increases uncertainty.

Primary outbreak data: Epidemiological context was drawn from publicly available reports: as of 4 June 2026, DRC had reported 363 confirmed cases including 62 deaths, and Uganda had reported 15 confirmed cases including one death. Because no airline-mediated importation had been reported as of 28 May 2026, no exported-case numerator was available to calibrate the current-outbreak hazard.

Auxiliary data: Airline passenger-flow data came from OAG (a global travel data provider listed in the IATA Strategic Partners Directory) for May 2024, covering 11,264 airports, 569,741 routes, and 329,326,078 passengers. The importation hazard parameter (θ = 0.19) was calibrated from the 2013–2016 West African EVD epidemic and applied here as the primary value. Epidemic growth rate estimates (r = {0.034, 0.05, 0.1}) used in sensitivity analyses were taken as of 17 May 2026.

hierarchy.lvl3: Epistorm (2026) Ebola Bundibugyo Virus — DRC · Spread Risk Assessment #4 · International Dissemination
Epistorm (2026) Ebola Bundibugyo Virus — DRC · Spread Risk Assessment #4 · International Dissemination

records.content: Read the report

This analysis quantifies relative importation risk in the immediate cross-border region around the affected provinces and across international destinations, ranking those destinations under a mobility-based framework. The modelling approach follows the GLEAM (Global Epidemic and Mobility) computational framework, estimating for each potential destination the probability that an infected individual originating in the affected area travels there, conditional on at least one exported case occurring, and producing a relative risk distribution normalised across all candidate destinations. The main finding is that conditional international risk concentrates almost entirely within Africa (98%), with Tanzania accounting for 45.3%, South Sudan for 23.4%, Kenya for 10.4%, and the UAE for 4.5% of that conditional international risk.

This analysis does not estimate outbreak size (total cases, infections, or deaths). It quantifies conditional relative importation risk to international destinations, given that at least one international export occurs, and is intended to indicate surveillance priority ranking among international destinations by relative likelihood. Absolute likelihood of any international export, downstream local transmission, and outbreak burden at source are explicitly out of scope.

The top-ranked destinations are Sumbawanga (TZA, RR 28.1%), Juba (SSD, RR 23.4%), Bukoba (TZA, RR 11.5%), Kisumu (KEN, RR 6.4%), and Dubai (ARE, RR 4.5%), together accounting for roughly 74% of the conditional international risk.

By region, the distribution is: Africa 84.5% (Tanzania 45.3%, South Sudan 23.4%, Kenya 10.4%, Ethiopia 1.1%); Middle East 7.3% (UAE 4.5%, Saudi Arabia 1.4%, Qatar 0.6%); Europe 3.7% (UK 1.2%, Germany 0.5%, Netherlands 0.3%); Asia 2.9% (India 1.2%, China 1.0%, Korea 0.1%); Americas 1.3% (USA 0.98%, Canada 0.36%); Other 0.2%.

These values are conditional on at least one international export occurring and describe how international exportation probability is distributed across destinations given that an international export happens, not the absolute probability that any specific destination will receive a case. The unconditional probability of international export remains small.

GLEAM mobility-based relative risk model: This computational framework estimates, for each potential destination, the probability that an infected individual originating in the affected area travels there, conditional on at least one exported case occurring. The output is a relative risk distribution across all candidate destinations, normalised so that values represent the share of importation probability assigned to each location.

Air travel and local mobility data integration: Global air travel patterns are derived from IATA and OAG, providing passenger-level origin-destination flows between international airports. These data are complemented by short-scale commuting patterns capturing daily movements between adjacent subpopulations, representing local spread through routine human activity within and across health zones.

Destination set restriction and renormalisation: Following the 2 June 2026 reopening of the Bunia International Airport, the model factors increased domestic and regional mobility from the affected area. To support surveillance prioritisation, the destination set is restricted to non-local international locations, excluding the affected area, other DRC locations, and Rwanda and Burundi, with relative risk values renormalised to sum to one across the 208 remaining international destinations. This approach is described as extending the same framework used in Reports #1 to #3 of this series.

Incubation period and travel window: The incubation period of Bundibugyo virus (2–21 days; average approximately 10 days) constrains the window within which an exported case could reasonably travel while infectious or pre-symptomatic. The model does not predict downstream transmission chains following an importation event, so risk estimates reflect only the probability of a single importation and do not account for the probability of sustained local transmission in the receiving location.

Conditionality: All values are conditional on at least one international export occurring; they describe how international exportation probability is distributed across destinations given that an international export happens, not the absolute probability that any specific destination receives a case.

Homogeneous traveller population: The mobility model does not incorporate sociodemographic attributes of travellers (age, economic status, occupation, or pre-existing medical conditions), any of which may modulate both exposure risk at origin and care-seeking behaviour at destination. Travel probability within a catchment area is treated as independent of these attributes and of specific location within the catchment, so estimates apply to traffic from the general population.

Exclusion of operational movements: The importation risk associated with repatriation flights, responder evacuations, or other ad-hoc operational movements is not part of this analysis.

Statistical independence of importation events: Case importations are modelled as statistically independent events. Real-world events involving multiple related cases (for example, a family cluster travelling together) are counted as a single importation event. To the extent that such clusters are common, the model will under-count the number of distinct exposure events while correctly counting the number of distinct geographic seedings.

Airport reopening traffic volumes: The reopening of Bunia Airport on 2 June 2026 is factored by resuming mobility from the region, although actual traffic may differ from pre-pandemic baselines.

Rwanda and Burundi as regional spillover: These two immediate regional neighbours are excluded from the international destination set on the assumption that their substantial mobility flows are better characterised as regional spillover than international dissemination, and because their border policy with respect to the outbreak remains uncertain.

Informal cross-border movement not captured: Mobility data may not fully capture informal cross-border movement, which is substantial within the immediate cross-border region. For the international tail considered in this report, the model relies on origin-destination flight data (IATA / OAG), which does not represent these informal flows.

Sociodemographic homogeneity: The mobility model does not incorporate sociodemographic attributes of travellers — age, economic status, occupation, or pre-existing medical conditions — any of which may modulate both exposure risk at origin and care-seeking behaviour at destination. Travel probability is treated as independent of these attributes and of specific location within the catchment.

Operational movements excluded: The importation risk associated with repatriation flights, responder evacuations, or other ad-hoc operational movements is not part of this analysis.

Cluster travel undercounted as distinct events: Case importations are modelled as statistically independent events, so real-world family clusters or group travel are counted as a single importation event. To the extent such clusters are common, the model will under-count distinct exposure events while correctly counting distinct geographic seedings.

Bunia airport baseline uncertainty: The Bunia Airport reopening is modelled by resuming mobility from the region, but actual traffic volumes may differ from the pre-pandemic baselines used.

No downstream transmission modelled: The model does not predict downstream transmission chains following an importation event; risk estimates reflect the probability of a single importation only and do not account for the probability of sustained local transmission in the receiving location.

Primary outbreak data: The analysis notes a confirmed CFR of approximately 14% (DRC/Uganda combined) for the current outbreak, with the pathogen identified as Bundibugyo virus. The source footprint is defined by 19 laboratory-confirmed cases in Uganda and a laboratory-confirmed travel-related case in Bukavu, South Kivu.

Auxiliary data — air travel: Global air travel patterns are derived from IATA and OAG, providing passenger-level origin-destination flows between international airports. Local mobility: Short-scale commuting patterns capturing daily movements between adjacent subpopulations are used to represent local spread through routine human activity within and across health zones. Incubation period parameters: The BVD incubation period is set at 2–21 days with an average of approximately 10 days, drawn from the 2007 outbreak.

hierarchy.lvl3: Richard et al. (2026) Assessment of Risk to the U.S. Population from the Ebola Disease Outbreak Caused by Bundibugyo Virus, 2026
Richard et al. (2026) Assessment of Risk to the U.S. Population from the Ebola Disease Outbreak Caused by Bundibugyo Virus, 2026

records.content: Read the report

In response to the BVD outbreak in DRC and Uganda, CDC assessed the risk posed by the outbreak to the U.S. population over the next 3 months, using a standardized risk assessment approach that drew on epidemiologic data from the ongoing outbreak and historical data from previous Ebola outbreaks. The overall risk was determined by independently assessing two factors: the likelihood of infection and the impact of infection. The assessment found that the overall risk to the U.S. population is low, based on an extremely low likelihood of transmission, despite the high impact that potential infection could have and the resources that would be required to respond.

The overall risk posed by the ongoing BVD outbreak to the U.S. population during the next 3 months is assessed as low, with moderate confidence given the data available. This overall risk reflects a combination of an extremely low likelihood of infection and a high impact of infection for the U.S. population were it to occur.

The relative risk of importation to the United States compared with other locations was estimated at 1.3%, derived from the population movement model. No quantitative uncertainty bounds are provided for this figure.

The impact of infection was assessed as high, primarily based on the severity of illness, lack of available medications and vaccines, and resources required to respond; in the two previously identified BVD outbreaks (Uganda 2007 and DRC 2012), case-fatality rates ranged from 25% to 50%.

No outbreak size estimate (total case count or under-detection factor) is reported. The analysis does not produce a central estimate with confidence intervals for total cases.

Standardized qualitative risk assessment framework: CDC subject matter experts in risk assessment methodology, infectious disease modeling, global health, and Ebola disease collaborated using a standardized risk assessment approach that has been applied to previous viral hemorrhagic fever outbreaks, drawing on available evidence including epidemiologic data from the ongoing BVD outbreak and historical data on Bundibugyo virus and other Ebola disease outbreaks. The overall risk to the U.S. population was determined by independently assessing two factors: the likelihood of infection (i.e., the probability that members of the U.S. population would acquire Bundibugyo virus infection during the next 3 months, depending on likelihood of exposure, infectiousness, and susceptibility) and the impact of infection (i.e., consequences including disease severity, population immunity, availability of medications and vaccines, and necessary public health response resources). Experts then assigned a degree of confidence to the assessment, taking into account evidence quality, extent, and corroboration of information.

Population movement model for importation risk: The likelihood of spread from DRC to the United States via infected travelers was assessed using a model that considers population movement; this produced a relative risk of importation to the United States compared with other locations of 1.3%. The method is described only briefly; no calibration procedure, parameter values, or data source for the mobility inputs are specified in the document.

Importation likelihood: The current likelihood of potential spread of BVD from DRC to the United States via travelers who might be infected is considered very low based on modeling results that consider population movement. The specific structural assumptions, parameter values, and data inputs underlying this model are not disclosed in the document.

Secondary transmission in the United States: If BVD were introduced into the United States, secondary transmission would likely be minimal, based on historical observation and the known epidemiology of BVD. This assumption relies on the known property that persons are not infectious before symptom onset and that the average interval between cases is long (10–16 days), making public health measures highly effective.

Population scope: Risk is assessed only for the general U.S. population, with subpopulations potentially having different likelihood assessments noted separately. Specifically, the likelihood of infection is judged to be higher among U.S. health care workers practicing in or recently returned from affected regions in DRC and Uganda.

Case fatality and clinical outcomes: CFRs of 25%–50% from previous BVD outbreaks are used to characterize disease severity, but the analysis notes that many of those deaths occurred in resource-limited settings and that clinical outcomes might improve with the specialized care available in the United States.

Outbreak trajectory: The assessment acknowledges uncertainties about the epidemiology of BVD, the scope and geographic spread of the outbreak, and the potential timelines for implementation of interventions, all of which represent assumptions embedded in the risk tier assigned.

Limited prior outbreak experience: Given that only two previous BVD outbreaks have occurred, less is known about Bundibugyo virus than other orthoebolaviruses such as O. zairense, which constrains the evidence base for the assessment.

Incomplete outbreak scope: Limited confirmatory diagnostic testing and challenges in contact tracing have likely resulted in undetected transmission, meaning the full scope of the current outbreak is unclear, making it difficult to assess the potential geographic spread of the outbreak and the subsequent potential for spread to the United States.

Operational and contextual challenges: Several features of this outbreak pose challenges to assessing its future trajectory, including that the outbreak is occurring in a region with ongoing conflict and unpredictable infrastructure, and the initial large size of the outbreak combined with limited confirmatory diagnostic testing may lead to challenges in infection prevention and control efforts.

General uncertainty: The assessment recognizes uncertainties around the epidemiology of BVD as well as the current and future scope and geographic spread of the outbreak.

Primary outbreak data: As of June 2, 2026, a total of 378 confirmed BVD cases and 63 deaths reported in DRC and Uganda, drawn from the two countries’ surveillance systems.

Auxiliary data: Historical data on Bundibugyo virus and other Ebola disease outbreaks were used to inform the assessment. Specifically, case-fatality rates from the two previous BVD outbreaks in Uganda (2007) and DRC (2012), ranging from 25% to 50%, were referenced. Historical observation of Ebola disease treatment and secondary transmission in the United States, specifically the 2014–2016 West African outbreak during which only 11 persons with Ebola disease were treated in the United States and no community spread occurred, was also used. Modeling results considering population movement informed the relative importation risk estimate of 1.3% for the United States compared with other locations. No specific dataset names, sample sizes for auxiliary sources, or mobility data sources are identified in the document.

hierarchy.lvl3: Fanelli et al. 2026 Shifting patterns of importation risk of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease to Europe under outbreak expansion scenarios
Fanelli et al. 2026 Shifting patterns of importation risk of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease to Europe under outbreak expansion scenarios

records.content: Read the report

Using International Air Transport Association (IATA) origin-destination passenger flows, this analysis estimates the relative risk of Bundibugyo virus disease importation into Europe under six outbreak expansion scenarios reflecting plausible pathways of geographical spread, including cross-border transmission and amplification in highly connected regional capitals. Relative exposure patterns across European countries remained largely unchanged under localized transmission in eastern DRC and border-spillover scenarios, but expansion into South Sudan generated a first structural increase in importation pressure to Europe, while hypothetical amplification in Kampala, Kigali, and Kinshasa substantially increased importation pressure and reshaped exposure patterns. Across all scenarios, France, Italy, and the United Kingdom remained among the most exposed countries.

This analysis does not produce an outbreak size estimate (i.e., a count of cases or infections). Its outputs are relative importation risk rankings for European countries and fold-change increases in importation pressure by European subregion under each scenario. Potential spread across the Ugandan border produced no change in either the relative exposure of European countries or their overall importation pressure compared with the currently reported localized transmission in eastern DRC, with Italy, France, Belgium, and the UK being the most exposed. Expansion to South Sudan produced a first structural increase in importation pressure through Juba (5-fold increase for Northern Europe and 6-fold increase for Eastern Europe relative to localized DRC transmission), while still leaving the UK, France, and Italy among the highest-risk countries. Kampala and Kigali amplification increased exposure in Northern Europe, with Kampala producing a >120-fold increase in importation pressure relative to localized DRC transmission; Kinshasa amplification primarily reinforced exposure in Western Europe (~80-fold increase). In the sensitivity analysis using full-year 2024 travel data, the Kampala amplification scenario produces approximately 190-fold increase for Northern Europe.

Air-travel importation risk model using IATA origin-destination flows: The analysis uses origin-destination passenger flows derived from the 2024 IATA database, which captures complete passenger itineraries including traffic travelling via connecting flights rather than only direct flight segments. The relative importation risk to each European country is estimated as the proportion of total outgoing passenger flow from the selected seed airports directed to that country, where passenger flow from each origin airport in a given scenario is summed across all destination airports in the receiving country, then normalized to total passenger flow from source airports to all of Europe. Risks are then stratified by European subregion. This approach produces relative (not absolute) importation risk rankings and does not incorporate incubation period, detection probability, or infectious prevalence at source. A sensitivity analysis using average monthly data from the full year 2024 was conducted to account for potential seasonal effects; results were broadly consistent with the main analysis, with the exception of the Kampala amplification scenario, in which the increase in importation pressure for Northern Europe rises to approximately 190-fold when using full-year monthly averages.

Proportional travel as a proxy for importation risk: The model assumes that the relative proportion of outbound passenger volume from seed airports to each European country is a valid proxy for relative importation risk. The relative importation risk to each destination country is estimated purely as the share of total outgoing passenger flow from the selected seed airports directed to that country, normalized to all Europe-bound flow. This structural assumption means that no adjustment is made for incubation period, probability of travelling while infectious, detection at exit or entry, or per-capita incidence at source — the analysis is explicitly relative, not absolute.

Scenario seeding and airport selection: The six scenarios are designed to reflect plausible pathways of geographical spread from the currently affected area through porous borders. Each scenario’s result depends entirely on which airports are included as seeds: adding or removing an airport changes the importation ranking. The analysis does not specify how the airport catchment areas were defined or how ground-to-air connectivity within source regions was accounted for.

Travel data representativeness: The analysis uses cumulative passenger traffic from May, June, and July 2024; this period showed moderate seasonal variability, with mean volumes ranging from 3% below to 16% above the monthly 2024 average across scenarios. The analysis implicitly assumes 2024 travel patterns are representative of 2026 patterns during the outbreak period, including any travel disruptions or demand changes associated with the outbreak itself.

Capital-city amplification as a hypothetical: Scenarios 4–6 explore hypothetical amplification assuming community transmission in major urban hubs — Kampala, Kigali, and Kinshasa. The analysis does not assign probabilities to these scenarios occurring; they are treated as illustrative of what importation pressure would look like if amplification did occur, rather than as probabilistic forecasts.

Relative risk only, no absolute importation probability: The analysis produces relative rankings and fold-change comparisons but does not estimate the absolute probability that any infected traveller departs or arrives at a given destination. Patterns of importation risk to Europe may rapidly shift under uncertainty surrounding the geographical extent and evolution of the outbreak, particularly if transmission expands into major urban hubs with strong international connectivity near the affected area. The analysis does not quantify this uncertainty in probabilistic terms.

Seasonal and temporal representativeness of travel data: The analyses use cumulative passenger traffic from May, June, and July 2024, a period that showed moderate seasonal variability relative to the 2024 annual average. The authors do not discuss whether outbreak-related changes in travel behaviour (e.g., reduced tourism or business travel to affected regions) would alter the estimates.

No adjustment for detection, infectious period, or per-capita incidence: The method does not incorporate incubation period distributions, probability of travelling while infectious, exit or entry screening effectiveness, or source-area incidence rates. As a consequence, all scenarios implicitly assume equal per-traveller exportation risk from each seed airport, which the authors do not explicitly flag as a limitation but is a direct consequence of the proportional-flow methodology.

Hypothetical nature of amplification scenarios: Scenarios 4–6 explore hypothetical amplification assuming community transmission in major urban hubs. The analysis does not assign likelihoods to these scenarios or model the transmission dynamics required to reach that state, limiting their direct operational applicability.

Primary outbreak data: As of 29 May 2026, the outbreak involved 125 confirmed cases (17 deaths) and 906 suspected cases (223 deaths) across Ituri, North Kivu, and South Kivu provinces in Eastern DRC, and 9 confirmed cases (1 death) in Uganda. These figures inform the scenario definitions (which areas to include as seeds) but are not directly used as a quantitative input to the model formula.

Auxiliary data: Origin-destination passenger flows are derived from the 2024 IATA database, which captures complete passenger itineraries including connecting flights. Analyses are based on cumulative passenger traffic reported for May, June, and July 2024. Relative to annual traffic to Europe, mean passenger volumes during May–July ranged from 3% below to 16% above the monthly 2024 average across scenarios. No line list, demographic data, or parameters borrowed from prior outbreaks are used as model inputs; the method relies solely on the IATA travel volumes and the airport-level scenario seeding definitions.

hierarchy.lvl3: van Zandvoort et al. (2026) The risk of global Ebola virus spread is low: epidemiology of Ebola disease cases outside Africa, 1976 to May 2026
van Zandvoort et al. (2026) The risk of global Ebola virus spread is low: epidemiology of Ebola disease cases outside Africa, 1976 to May 2026

records.content: Read the report

This analysis reviews the historical record of confirmed Ebola disease cases presenting outside Africa to contextualize the intercontinental spread risk posed by the 2026 Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) outbreak in DRC. The authors conducted internet searches — both manual and AI-assisted — to identify all laboratory-confirmed Ebola disease cases presenting outside Africa, without any time limit. The main finding is that intercontinental transmission risk remains low, based on the full historical record of such cases.

The analysis does not produce an outbreak size estimate for the 2026 BVD outbreak itself. Instead it reports importation risk metrics derived from the historical record.

The crude overall risk since 2000 is 0.81 cases outside Africa per 1,000 Ebola disease cases in a source outbreak (including medically evacuated, latent, and secondary cases); focusing on latent cases and their secondary cases only, the rate is 0.17 cases outside Africa per 1,000 source outbreak cases. This represents the empirically observed risk across all countries outside Africa combined; per-country risks are smaller — for example, for the UK alone, the estimate is 0.03 latent cases per 1,000 source outbreak cases.

From the time-varying analysis of the 2014–16 epidemic, exportation risk per reported source case decreased by 4.6% per week (95% CI: 1.7–7.4%; p = 0.0097), and was substantially lower after the epidemic peak (0.46; 95% CI: 0.17–0.73 exported cases per 1,000 source cases) than before it (1.7; 95% CI: 0.95–2.7 exported cases per 1,000 source cases).

Historical case review and crude risk calculation: The authors identified and analysed all known Ebola disease cases outside Africa to contextualise intercontinental spread, using manual and AI-assisted internet searches of scientific articles, public health bulletins, and news reports. As a crude risk measure, they calculated the number of cases occurring outside Africa per 1,000 reported cases in the originating epidemic for outbreaks since 2000, producing separate estimates including and excluding medically evacuated cases. This calculation applies globally across all destination countries outside Africa combined, with a UK-specific example provided at the national level.

Time-varying Poisson regression: For cases exported from the 2014–16 West African epidemic specifically, the authors conducted a time-varying risk analysis using Poisson regression of the weekly count of exported cases by country (Guinea, Liberia, Sierra Leone) against the number of cases reported that week and a linear time trend. This produced an estimate of how exported case counts scaled with source-country incidence, with no significant deviation from linear scaling found. This method applies to the 2014–16 epidemic as a historical comparator and is not applied directly to the 2026 BVD outbreak.

Linear scaling of exportation risk with source incidence: The Poisson regression assumes that exported case counts scale linearly with weekly source-country incidence, an assumption the analysis found consistent with the 2014–16 data (estimated scaling: 0.98; 95% CI: 0.60–1.36; p = 0.86). This assumption underpins the use of crude per-1,000-cases risk ratios to project forward.

Crude risk ratio as applicable across outbreaks: The crude risk measure is calculated for Ebola disease outbreaks since the year 2000, implicitly assuming that the historical ratio of exported-to-source cases is informative for the current 2026 BVD outbreak. The analysis does not explicitly state whether this assumption holds given differences in travel patterns, response capacity, or the specific virus species involved.

Distinction between medically evacuated and latent cases: The analysis distinguishes between medically evacuated cases, who represent known risks manageable with strict biosecurity, and latent cases arriving via commercial flights who need to be diagnosed before isolation. The risk ratios are computed separately for these two categories, with the latent-only figure treated as the more policy-relevant measure of uncontrolled importation risk.

Completeness of historical case ascertainment: Agreement among search methods and with a prior clinical review supports that the case list is likely complete, but potential under-ascertainment may arise from underrepresentation of public health bulletins not published in English, and from possible geographic biases in web-based search tools.

Potential under-ascertainment of exported cases: Public health bulletins not published in English were underrepresented despite targeted native-language searches, and web-based search tools may have returned geographically biased results, potentially leading to missed cases.

Historical data may not reflect current outbreak conditions: The analysis applies risk ratios derived primarily from the 2014–16 West African epidemic — a very large, prolonged epidemic with substantial international humanitarian response — to the 2026 BVD context. The authors note that the decrease in exportation risk over the 2014–16 epidemic is likely most consistent with improvement in infection prevention and control measures coinciding with the stepping-up of response efforts in late 2014, but do not formally assess whether this dynamic applies to the current, structurally different outbreak.

Per-country risks not estimated: The crude risk estimate represents the empirically observed risk across all countries outside Africa; per-country risks are smaller, and the analysis provides only the aggregate outside-Africa figure plus a single UK example, without ranking or estimating risk for individual destination countries.

Latent case detection depends on screening: The four latent cases identified occurred among 300,000 screened travellers and were all asymptomatic at exit and entry screening; their eventual identification relied on screening protocols that included guidance on self-monitoring of symptoms. The analysis does not assess what the latent case rate would have been in the absence of such protocols.

Primary outbreak data: The analysis identified 28 confirmed Ebola disease cases outside Africa in total, of which 27 occurred during the 2014–16 West African epidemic and one has occurred so far during the 2026 BVD epidemic. Sources consulted included 27 scientific articles, 9 public health bulletins, 82 news articles, and 9 other sites.

Auxiliary data: Weekly case counts for Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone (total 28,592 confirmed, probable, and suspected cases) covering the 2014–16 epidemic were used as the source-country incidence series in the Poisson regression. The authors also reference the approximately 300,000 travellers screened by CDC-supported exit screening programmes in Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone during the 2014–16 epidemic, as context for the four identified latent cases. Findings were checked against existing lists of Ebola disease cases.
